rounds.about: One of Britain's most historic circuits, dating back to 1931. The National layout features the infamous Craner Curves — a sweeping downhill chicane that rewards commitment — alongside the Old Hairpin and the fast Schwantz Curve. The circuit also hosted a famous Formula 1 European Grand Prix in 1993, remembered for Ayrton Senna's extraordinary opening lap.
rounds.btccFact: Donington opens the BTCC season almost every year, meaning the winner of the first race here is often considered the early championship favourite.

corners.name: Redgate
corners.description: Sharp right-hander at the end of the start/finish straight. The prime overtaking zone on lap one and throughout the race.

corners.name: Hollywood
corners.description: Fast right-hand kink through the trees, taken flat out. Sets up the entry into Craner Curves.

corners.name: Craner Curves
corners.description: Iconic downhill sweeping left-hand sequence. One of the most thrilling corners in British motorsport — requires full commitment at high speed.

corners.name: Old Hairpin
corners.description: Tight right-hand hairpin at the bottom of the hill. A classic late-braking overtaking spot with a wide entry.

corners.name: McLeans
corners.description: Right-hander at the end of Starkey's Straight. Heavy braking zone with overtaking possible on the inside.

corners.name: Coppice
corners.description: Fast right-hand bend taken in a high gear. Rewards bravery and a late apex sets up the run to the final corner.

corners.name: Goddards
corners.description: Tight right-hand chicane complex, the final braking zone before the pit straight. Last chance to make a pass each lap.

rounds.about: The short, intense Indy loop of the legendary Brands Hatch circuit. Paddock Hill Bend is one of the most famous corners in British motorsport — blind, downhill, and off-camber — providing spectacular action on the opening lap. The tight Druids hairpin and Clark Curve create close-quarters racing throughout the field.
rounds.btccFact: The BTCC visits Brands Hatch twice each season — on the shorter Indy circuit in spring and the full Grand Prix layout for the season finale in October.

corners.name: Paddock Hill Bend
corners.description: Iconic blind right-hander dropping steeply downhill with off-camber exit. One of the most famous corners in British motorsport — spectacular on the opening lap.

corners.name: Druids
corners.description: Tight left-hand hairpin at the top of the hill. The prime overtaking spot — heavy braking and a wide entry invite late moves.

corners.name: Graham Hill Bend
corners.description: Right-hander on the descent from Druids. Getting this right is key to carrying speed onto Cooper Straight.

corners.name: Surtees
corners.description: Fast right-hand kink at the bottom of the hill. Taken at high speed, the dip loads the car before turning in.

corners.name: Pilgrims Drop
corners.description: Left-hand kink over a crest. The car goes light, making the rear end lively.

corners.name: Clearways
corners.description: Long right-hand sweeping complex. Commitment through here sets up exit speed onto the pit straight.

corners.name: Clark Curve
corners.description: Final right-hander back onto the start/finish straight. A good exit is crucial for a run into Paddock Hill on the next lap.

rounds.about: Built on a former Second World War airfield in the flat Norfolk countryside, Snetterton is one of the longest circuits on the BTCC calendar. The Bentley Straight offers tyre-testing high-speed running, while technical sections like the Senna Chicane and Coram Corner reward smooth, committed driving. Setup compromise between straight-line speed and mechanical grip is key.
rounds.btccFact: Snetterton's long straights mean it's one of the circuits where power advantage makes the biggest difference — teams running higher-powered cars often shine here.

corners.name: Riches
corners.description: Right-hander at the end of the long Bentley Straight. Heavy braking zone and a prime overtaking opportunity.

corners.name: Montreal
corners.description: Tight left-hand hairpin. Slow speed and a wide entry make this another overtaking spot.

corners.name: Palmer
corners.description: Right-hander that feeds onto the back section of the circuit.

corners.name: Agostini
corners.description: Right-hand bend leading into Hamilton.

corners.name: Hamilton
corners.description: Left-hander that completes the complex before the run to the Senna Chicane.

corners.name: Oggies/Williams
corners.description: Right-left flick through a fast section. Smooth inputs are essential.

corners.name: Senna Chicane
corners.description: Tight right-left chicane named after the late Ayrton Senna. A key overtaking zone under braking.

corners.name: Coram
corners.description: Long, fast right-hand sweeper. One of the most demanding corners — rewards commitment and a precise line.

corners.name: Russell
corners.description: Right-hander linking Coram to the final section.

corners.name: Bomb Hole
corners.description: Left-hand dip through a compression. The car bottoms out, making it tricky under braking for the pit straight.

rounds.about: A consistent driver favourite. Set in the rolling Cheshire countryside, Oulton Park combines sweeping fast corners with elevation changes that put the car through its paces. Cascades is a breathtaking flat-out downhill right-hander, while the Knickerbrook complex and the Lodge chicane reward precision. It's the type of track that exposes both talent and machinery.
rounds.btccFact: Oulton Park has produced some of the most memorable BTCC moments of recent seasons, with its undulating nature leading to unpredictable results and dramatic incidents.

corners.name: Old Hall
corners.description: Right-hander off the start/finish straight. Tight and technical — a frequent overtaking spot on lap one.

corners.name: Dentons
corners.description: Left-hand kink that leads downhill into the Cascades section.

corners.name: Cascades
corners.description: Fast right-hander dropping steeply downhill. One of the most spectacular corners on the calendar — taken at high speed with limited visibility.

corners.name: Island Bend
corners.description: Tight left-hand hairpin at the bottom of the hill. Heavy braking and a wide entry make it a key overtaking zone.

corners.name: Shell Oils
corners.description: Right-hander climbing back uphill.

corners.name: Brittens
corners.description: Left-hand chicane that rewards precision and a late apex.

corners.name: Hislops
corners.description: Right-hand bend feeding onto the run to Knickerbrook.

corners.name: Knickerbrook
corners.description: Right-hand chicane complex with a tricky change of direction. Gets the car unsettled.

corners.name: Druids
corners.description: Left-hander that sets up the braking zone for Lodge.

corners.name: Lodge
corners.description: Right-left chicane. A hard braking zone with overtaking possible into the right-hander.

corners.name: Deer Leap
corners.description: Right-hand kink over a crest — the car goes light and the rear steps out.

corners.name: Clay Hill
corners.description: Left-hander on the descent back to the pit straight. A good exit is vital for a run into Old Hall.

rounds.about: Britain's fastest circuit, built on a wartime airfield in Hampshire. Wide, fast, and almost entirely flat, Thruxton is a circuit where bravery is rewarded. The sweeping Cobb–Noble–Church complex is taken at high speed in a single flowing line, while Campbell/Coppice is among the most demanding high-speed sequences in domestic racing.
rounds.btccFact: Average lap speeds at Thruxton are the highest of any BTCC venue, regularly exceeding 120 mph. It's a true driver's circuit where mechanical sympathy under sustained high loads is paramount.

corners.name: Allard
corners.description: Right-hand kink taken flat out off the start/finish straight.

corners.name: Campbell/Cobb
corners.description: Fast right-left sweeping complex taken at enormous speed. One of the most demanding sequences in the BTCC — tyre wear here is critical.

corners.name: Segrave
corners.description: Left-hand kink linking the high-speed section to Noble.

corners.name: Noble
corners.description: Right-hander that feeds into the Church complex at high speed.

corners.name: Church
corners.description: Fast right-hand sweeper. Taken in a high gear — commitment through here defines the lap.

corners.name: Club
corners.description: Right-hand hairpin and the main overtaking zone. The only heavy braking point on the lap.

corners.name: Woodham Hill
corners.description: Left-hander climbing uphill. A tricky corner that punishes entry speed.

corners.name: Goodwood
corners.description: Right-left chicane before the pit straight. A good exit here sets up a slipstream opportunity.

rounds.about: Scotland's National Motorsport Centre, tucked into the Fife hills. Compact and technically demanding, Knockhill is unlike any other circuit on the calendar — the undulating gradient means cars are constantly loaded and unloaded through corners, making the rear of a touring car particularly lively. The blind crest at Clark's Corner demands total commitment.
rounds.btccFact: The Knockhill crowd is famously passionate, and the circuit's compact nature means fans can see virtually the entire lap from the hillside spectator banks — making it a fantastic atmosphere for a race meeting.

corners.name: Duffus Dip
corners.description: Right-hander dropping steeply downhill into a compression. The car is heavily loaded on entry.

corners.name: Leslie's
corners.description: Left-hand hairpin at the lowest point of the circuit. The tightest corner on the track and the primary overtaking zone.

corners.name: Scotsman
corners.description: Right-hander climbing back uphill.

corners.name: Clark's
corners.description: Left-hander over a blind crest. One of the most daunting corners on the BTCC calendar — total commitment required with zero visibility.

corners.name: Railway
corners.description: Right-left complex on the run down to the chicane.

corners.name: McIntyres
corners.description: Right-hand hairpin. A heavy braking zone that offers overtaking opportunities.

corners.name: Chicane
corners.description: Left-right chicane feeding onto the uphill section.

corners.name: Buttermilk
corners.description: Right-left sequence on the climb back to the top of the circuit.

corners.name: Taylor's
corners.description: Right-hand kink complex leading back onto the start/finish straight.

rounds.about: The full Grand Prix layout extends the National circuit with the Melbourne Loop and the Foggy/Goddards complex, significantly increasing lap length and introducing additional technical challenge. The extra section adds meaningful overtaking opportunities and changes the strategic picture for tyre management. It last hosted a Formula 1 race in 1993.
rounds.btccFact: Donington GP provides a fascinating contrast with Round 1 at the same venue — drivers who struggled on the National loop earlier in the season can find the extra GP section suits their driving style far better.

corners.name: Redgate
corners.description: Sharp right-hander at the end of the start/finish straight. Same as the National circuit — prime overtaking zone.

corners.name: Hollywood
corners.description: Fast right-hand kink through the trees, taken flat out.

corners.name: Craner Curves
corners.description: Iconic downhill sweeping left-hand sequence. Full commitment at high speed.

corners.name: Old Hairpin
corners.description: Tight right-hand hairpin. A classic late-braking overtaking spot.

corners.name: McLeans
corners.description: Right-hander at the end of Starkey's Straight.

corners.name: Coppice
corners.description: Fast right-hand bend. On the GP layout this leads to the Melbourne Loop rather than Goddards.

corners.name: Melbourne Hairpin
corners.description: Tight left-hand hairpin added by the GP extension. A heavy braking zone and major overtaking opportunity.

corners.name: Melbourne Loop
corners.description: Right-left complex through the GP extension. A technical section that changes the character of the lap.

corners.name: Goddards
corners.description: Tight right-hand chicane complex, the final braking zone before the pit straight.

rounds.about: A classic British club circuit with roots going back to the 1960s, built on a former RAF airfield in North Yorkshire. Croft combines fast sections with tight chicanes and the iconic Tower Hairpin — one of the best overtaking spots on the calendar. Its relative geographical isolation gives it a distinct, grassroots motorsport atmosphere.
rounds.btccFact: Tower Hairpin at Croft is consistently one of the best places for late-braking overtakes in the BTCC, and it regularly produces race-defining moments.

corners.name: Clervaux
corners.description: Fast right-hander at the end of the start/finish straight. A brave entry is rewarded.

corners.name: Hawthorn
corners.description: Left-right chicane. Unsettles the car and rewards smooth inputs.

corners.name: Tower Bend
corners.description: Right-hander leading into the Tower complex.

corners.name: Tower Hairpin
corners.description: Tight right-hand hairpin. One of the best overtaking spots on the entire BTCC calendar — heavy braking from high speed.

corners.name: Jim Clark Esses
corners.description: Left-right-left S-curves. A fast, flowing sequence that demands rhythm and precision.

corners.name: Sunny In
corners.description: Left-hander entering the Sunny complex.

corners.name: Sunny Out
corners.description: Right-hander exiting the complex.

corners.name: Complex
corners.description: Right-hand bend leading towards Barcroft.

corners.name: Barcroft
corners.description: Right-hand hairpin. A late-braking zone with overtaking possible.

corners.name: Chicane
corners.description: Final chicane before the pit straight. A good exit sets up a run into Clervaux.

rounds.about: The home of British motorsport. Silverstone's Grand Prix circuit is the longest and fastest on the BTCC calendar, demanding aerodynamic efficiency and high-speed confidence throughout its many quick corners. Maggotts–Becketts–Chapel is one of the great sequences in world motorsport, taken flat in qualifying trim. Abbey, The Loop, and Village provide overtaking opportunities.
rounds.btccFact: Silverstone is where championship battles are frequently decided. The points available at this penultimate round mean that going into the Brands Hatch finale with a significant lead — or deficit — can completely shape the outcome.

corners.name: Abbey
corners.description: Fast right-hander at the end of the Wellington Straight. A key overtaking zone under braking.

corners.name: Farm
corners.description: Right-hand curve linking Abbey to Village.

corners.name: Village
corners.description: Right-hand corner leading into The Loop.

corners.name: The Loop
corners.description: Tight left-hand hairpin. The slowest point on the lap and a prime overtaking spot.

corners.name: Aintree
corners.description: Right-hander exiting the Loop complex.

corners.name: Wellington Straight
corners.description: Not a corner but the main overtaking straight — slipstreaming and DRS-style runs into Brooklands.

corners.name: Brooklands
corners.description: Right-hander at the end of Wellington Straight.

corners.name: Luffield
corners.description: Left-hand hairpin. Slow speed corner where traction on exit is crucial.

corners.name: Woodcote
corners.description: Right-hand kink, fast, leading to Copse.

corners.name: Copse
corners.description: Fast right-hander. One of the great corners — demands commitment at very high speed.

corners.name: Maggotts-Becketts-Chapel
corners.description: Iconic left-right-left high-speed esses. One of the greatest corner sequences in world motorsport — taken near flat out in qualifying.

corners.name: Stowe
corners.description: Right-hander at the end of Hangar Straight. Heavy braking from very high speed — overtaking is common here.

corners.name: Vale
corners.description: Left-hander that feeds into the Club complex.

corners.name: Club
corners.description: Right-hand complex, the final corners of the lap. A good exit is essential for the run back to Abbey.

rounds.about: The season finale. The full Grand Prix circuit adds Hawthorns, Westfield, Dingle Dell, and Stirlings to the Indy loop, creating one of the most spectacular circuits in Britain. Paddock Hill Bend on the opening lap of the finale race has been the scene of some of the most dramatic championship-deciding moments in BTCC history.
rounds.btccFact: A Brands Hatch GP BTCC finale is one of the highlights of the British motorsport calendar. With reverse-grid races and three separate races across the weekend, the championship result is rarely decided until the very last lap.

corners.name: Paddock Hill Bend
corners.description: Iconic blind right-hander dropping steeply downhill. Even more dramatic on the GP layout with the championship on the line.

corners.name: Druids
corners.description: Tight left-hand hairpin at the top of the hill. Prime overtaking spot.

corners.name: Graham Hill Bend
corners.description: Right-hander on the descent from Druids.

corners.name: Surtees
corners.description: Fast right-hand kink at the bottom of the hill.

corners.name: Hawthorns
corners.description: Left-hand bend entering the GP extension. A commitment corner through the trees.

corners.name: Westfield
corners.description: Right-hander dropping downhill. The car is unsettled by the gradient change.

corners.name: Dingle Dell
corners.description: Left-hand dip through a compression — the car bottoms out making it tricky.

corners.name: Dingle Dell Corner
corners.description: Right-hander climbing back out of the dell.

corners.name: Stirlings
corners.description: Right-hand bend rejoining the Indy loop. A key corner for exit speed.

corners.name: Clearways
corners.description: Long right-hand sweeper. Commitment here sets up exit speed onto the pit straight.

corners.name: Clark Curve
corners.description: Final right-hander back onto the start/finish straight. With the championship at stake, every tenth counts here.
